content.text: User Authentication
Every agentic API that acts on behalf of a real user needs authentication: the ability to verify
who is making the request and to scope what the agent is allowed to do. Getting this right
matters more than in a typical CRUD API because agents can take sequences of actions — a single
authenticated call may trigger many downstream operations. This sub-subtopic covers JWT verification,
OAuth 2.0 token exchange, API key validation, and how to pass identity securely into the agent loop.

attrs.alt: User Authentication Flow for Agentic APIs

content.text: JWT Verification Middleware
JSON Web Tokens are the most common authentication mechanism for agentic REST APIs.
The middleware extracts the Bearer token, verifies the signature against the IdP's public key,
and attaches the decoded claims to the request — downstream route handlers and the agent itself
never touch raw tokens.

# pip install fastapi python-jose[cryptography] httpx
import os, httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException, Request
from jose import JWTError, jwt
from functools import lru_cache
from dataclasses import dataclass

JWKS_URI = os.environ["JWKS_URI"]   # e.g. https://auth.example.com/.well-known/jwks.json
AUDIENCE = os.environ["JWT_AUDIENCE"]
ISSUER   = os.environ["JWT_ISSUER"]

@lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def get_jwks() -> dict:
    # Called once; cache for the process lifetime.
    # In production, refresh periodically or on 401 from verify().
    resp = httpx.get(JWKS_URI, timeout=5)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

@dataclass
class UserContext:
    sub: str          # user id
    email: str
    roles: list[str]  # ["admin", "analyst", ...]

async def require_user(request: Request) -> UserContext:
    auth = request.headers.get("Authorization", "")
    if not auth.startswith("Bearer "):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Missing Bearer token")
    token = auth.split(" ", 1)[1]
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(
            token,
            get_jwks(),
            algorithms=["RS256"],
            audience=AUDIENCE,
            issuer=ISSUER,
        )
    except JWTError as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail=f"Invalid token: {e}")
    return UserContext(
        sub=payload["sub"],
        email=payload.get("email", ""),
        roles=payload.get("roles", []),
    )

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/agent/chat")
async def agent_chat(body: dict, user: UserContext = Depends(require_user)):
    # user.sub, user.email, user.roles are now available
    tools = get_tools_for_user(user)
    response = run_agent(body["message"], tools=tools, user=user)
    return {"response": response}
OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange
When your agent backend needs to call third-party APIs (GitHub, Google, Salesforce) on behalf of
the user, you need their OAuth access token — not your service credentials. The client-side OAuth
flow obtains the token; the backend receives it and uses it for downstream calls.
API Key Authentication
For server-to-server agents or developer tooling, API keys are simpler than OAuth.
The pattern: hash the key before storing, look it up on every request, attach the
associated principal to the request context.

import hashlib, secrets, os
from fastapi import Header, HTTPException
from dataclasses import dataclass

# In production use a real database, not a dict
# Keys are stored as SHA-256 hashes; the raw key is shown once at creation time.
_KEY_STORE: dict[str, dict] = {}  # hash -> {"tenant_id": ..., "roles": [...]}

def create_api_key(tenant_id: str, roles: list[str]) -> str:
    """Generate a new API key and store its hash. Returns the raw key (show once)."""
    raw_key = "sk-" + secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    key_hash = hashlib.sha256(raw_key.encode()).hexdigest()
    _KEY_STORE[key_hash] = {"tenant_id": tenant_id, "roles": roles}
    return raw_key  # caller must store this; we never store it

@dataclass
class APIKeyContext:
    tenant_id: str
    roles: list[str]

def require_api_key(x_api_key: str = Header(...)) -> APIKeyContext:
    key_hash = hashlib.sha256(x_api_key.encode()).hexdigest()
    entry = _KEY_STORE.get(key_hash)
    if entry is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid API key")
    return APIKeyContext(tenant_id=entry["tenant_id"], roles=entry["roles"])

# FastAPI route using API key auth:
# @app.post("/agent/run")
# async def run(body: dict, ctx: APIKeyContext = Depends(require_api_key)):
#     tools = get_tools_for_roles(ctx.roles)
#     return run_agent(body["prompt"], tools=tools, tenant_id=ctx.tenant_id)
Role-Based Tool Scoping
Authentication tells you who the user is; authorisation determines what tools the agent can
call on their behalf. Scoping tools to roles is the simplest way to enforce least privilege:
pass only the permitted tool definitions to the model so it never even sees restricted tools.
Passing Identity Through the Agent Loop
The model itself must not be trusted to enforce auth — tool execution happens in your Python
code, not in the model. The pattern: authenticate once at the API boundary,
attach the user context to every tool call, and verify inside each tool.
Token Caching and Refresh
Verifying a JWT on every request requires fetching the IdP's JWKS endpoint. Cache the JWKS
with a short TTL (5–15 minutes) so you don't hammer the IdP. If a token fails verification
with cached keys, invalidate the cache and retry once — the IdP may have rotated keys.
Auth Method Comparison
Method
Best For
Token Lifetime
User Identity
JWT (RS256)
End-user web/mobile clients
Short (15m–1h), refresh via refresh token
Sub + email + roles in claims
OAuth 2.0 Access Token
Third-party API delegation
Provider-controlled (1h typical)
Verified via introspection / userinfo endpoint
API Key
Server-to-server, developer tooling
Long-lived (revoke on compromise)
Looked up from store; attached principal
mTLS Client Cert
Internal microservice auth
Cert validity period
Common Name (CN) of client cert
Common Pitfalls
Trusting the model to enforce permissions.
The model decides which tool to call, not whether the caller is allowed. Always verify inside the tool execution code.
Storing raw API keys.
Store only the SHA-256 hash; return the raw key once at creation. Exposure of the hash store is then harmless.
No token expiry check.
python-jose
checks expiry automatically when you pass
options={"verify_exp": True}
(the default). Disable this only in tests.
JWKS fetched on every request.
One JWKS fetch per request adds 20–100ms latency and hammers the IdP under load. Use a process-level cache with TTL and force-refresh on key rotation.
Leaking user data across sessions.
If you cache agent state (conversation history) by session ID, ensure session IDs are cryptographically random and tied to the authenticated user — never re-use across users.
Summary
User authentication for agentic APIs follows the same principles as standard REST auth but requires
extra care because agents call tools in sequence. Authenticate at the API boundary with JWT or OAuth;
scope tools to the user's roles at request time; verify permissions again inside each tool execution;
cache JWKS keys with TTL to avoid hammering the IdP. API keys work well for server-to-server agents —
store only hashes, never raw keys.
